g.data-narration: Welcome to Java Collection Interfaces - the foundation of data handling in Java.
g.text: Understanding the Foundation of Data Handling

g.data-narration: Collections provide standardized ways to store, organize, and manipulate groups of objects efficiently.
g.text: • Used everywhere in Java: user records, unique tags, message buffering
• Foundation for ArrayList, HashSet, HashMap, and enterprise applications
• Prerequisite for Iterators, Comparators, and Collection Algorithms

g.data-narration: The Collection hierarchy starts with the root Collection interface, branching into List, Set, and Queue.
The root Collection interface provides fundamental methods like add, remove, contains, and size.
g.text: The root interface with basic operations inherited by all collections:
add(e) remove(e) contains(e) size() isEmpty() clear() iterator()
Basic building block for all collections

g.data-narration: List is an ordered collection that allows duplicates and provides index-based access via get, set, and add methods.
g.text: List Interface - Ordered Collection with Duplicates

g.data-narration: Lists support duplicates and index-based access - you can retrieve elements by position using get method.
Set is a collection with no duplicates - it enforces uniqueness using equals and hashCode methods.
Queue is a FIFO collection designed for holding elements prior to processing with offer, poll, and peek methods.
g.text: Queue Interface - First In, First Out

g.data-narration: Deque supports insertion and removal at both ends, enabling LIFO (stack) and FIFO (queue) operations.
g.text: Can act as Stack (LIFO) or Queue (FIFO)

g.data-narration: Map is NOT part of Collection hierarchy - it stores key-value pairs with unique keys for fast lookups.
g.text: Map Interface - Key-Value Pairs (NOT in Collection hierarchy!)

g.data-narration: Iterable enables enhanced for-loops and provides iterator method for traversing collections.
Fail-fast iterators throw ConcurrentModificationException when collection is structurally modified during iteration.
Remember to override equals and hashCode for custom objects in collections, and use Comparable for natural ordering.
Review the key differences: List allows duplicates and indexing, Set ensures uniqueness, Queue is FIFO, Map stores key-value pairs.
Master these interfaces to choose the right data structure, write efficient Java code, and build scalable applications.
g.text: Map is separate from Collection • Use List for ordered duplicates • Use Set for uniqueness • Queue for FIFO processing
Choose the right interface for your data needs!
